Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance

Dear I'1r . Brown:
You are seeking general advj-ce on behalf of nembers of the
City of Berkeley Pl-anning Cornrnission ("the commission'r), regarding
their duties and responsibi l ities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the rrn"irr; .1 we provide
you with advice as the authorized representative of the
commission, pursuant to Regulation 18329 (copy enclosed) - As your
request for advice does not refer to a specific pending
governmental decision, ve are treating your request as one for
informal assistance on1y. (Regulation 1-8329(b) .)z
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
Ietter of January 10, 1991 and subsequent telephone conversations
$/ith Sarah Reynoso of your office on January 30, 199L.

one or more members of the City of Berkeley Planning
Comrnission may osrn proPerty located within one of the city's
zoning distrilts

Foreseeabte ltaterial FinaDcial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it $iiII occur. while certainty
is not required, an effect that is merefy a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Do$/ney cares v. Downev communitv
Developnent Com. (1987) 196 CaI.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC ops. 198.) The Act, however, does seek to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(Witt v. Morro!,
(1,97'7) 7O cal-.App.3d 817, 823.)
(1) Plannincr commissioner who owns propertv within a citv
zoninq district:
Decisions reqardinq the zoninq district
A vote to change the classification of a zoning district
appears to meet the reasonably foreseeabte threshold. 
Depending
on the change, greater burdens or fewer restrictions on the
properties owned within the zoning district wilL result.
In order to determine whether this foreseeable effect is
rnaterial as to a commissioner's real property interest when he or
she ovrns property in a zoning district which is the subject of a
zoning cllssification 
decision, Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed)
is applicable. In part, this regulation provides that the effect
of a decision is material if:
Ihe decision involves the zoning or rezoDittg,
annexation or deannexation, sale purchase or
lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any
city, county, district or other loca1
governmental subdivision, of real Property ir
ibich tle official 
has a dlirect or iadirect
interest (other than a leashold interest) of
$r,ooo or nore, or a similar decision
affecting such ProPerty.
(Regulation La702.1 (a) (3) (A), ernphasis
added. )
I.tateriality is thus presumed. Accordingly, a commissi,oner
nay g;neralfy nirt particilate in decisions regarding zonilg
districts 
$rhen he or she bwttt ptop"tty within the zoning district'

general public; the decision must affect the official's 
interest
in substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public. 
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed.)
Because all residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of
the official, 
these residents in the agency's jurisdiction
comprise the I'pub1ic.'r (fn re Lecran (1985) 9 FPPC ops. 1,' In re
Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copies enclosed.) For the public
generally exception to apply to the issue you have raised, a
decision on the zoning district 
rnust irnpact a significant 
segrment
of the population of the city of Berkeley in substantially the
same manner as it would the individual planning commissioner.
(Hirsch Advice Letter, No. A-90-195, copy encl-osed.) This
requires analysis of, and j-s obviously dependent upon, the facts
particular to the situation.
Deci-sions reqardinq nearbv zoninq districts
Because the analysis of this issue is fact specific, we
provide you only with the following general guidance. Depending
on the facts, a vote to change the classification of one zoning
district 
may reasonably and foreseeably irnpact properties located
in an adjacent or nearby zoning district. 
Greater burdens or
fewer restrictions on the properties owned within one zoning
district 
may very well result in irnpacts on other properties in a
nearby or adjacent district.
In such situations the official's 
real property j'nterests are
indirectly involved in the decision, and nateriality
deterrninalions are subject to analysis under Regulation 18702.3
(copy enctosed). In Part, this regulation provides that tt]e
det-eirnination of whether the effect of a decision i-s material
depends on the distance between the official's 
real property and
thl boundaries of the Property which is the subject of tt'e
decision.3 The speciflc facts of the zoning classification,
decision and the application of the facts to the regrulation's
criteria determine ihether a disqualifying conflict results'

if the decision before the planning commission is one to amend an
existing zoning ordinance or other land use regulation, in a man-
ner applicable to all properties in the city that are_designated
in the- zoning category. For example, a planning commission deci-

district. 
(Regulation La702.1(a) (3) (E) ')
(2)
of a non-profit corporation which owns propertv

tion which has an interest in reaL property within a city zoning
district 
does not, by itself, 
create a disqualifying conflict for

only if the non-profit corporation constitutes a source of

will

pertils owned witfrin the zoning district.

this-regulation provides that the effect of the decision is

Regulation La702.5 applies various financial inpact
thresholds, depending on the gross annual receipts of the non-
profit entity. 
I{hether the effect of a zoning decision will be
iraterial depends on the facts specific to the decision. 
A
commissioner who appeared to have a disqualifying confLict under

if applicatj-on of the "public generally exception'r (described

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
